---
format:
  revealjs:
    title-slide: false
    theme: black
    slide-number: true
    transition: fade
    background-transition: fade
    incremental: false
    embed-resources: true
    center: true
    controls: true
    progress: true
    code-overflow: wrap
  
css:
  - ../slide_theme/custom.scss

editor_options:
  chunk_output_type: console
---
## {background-color="#111111"}

<div class="cover-slide">

<div class="cover-image">
![](../Project_Image/peru_land_cover.gif)
</div>

<div class="cover-text">
<h1>Peru Land Cover Change Analysis</h1>
<p class="cover-subtitle">Spatial Data Analysis Final Project</p>
<p class="cover-author">Dylan Gong</p>
</div>

</div>

## Why I Chose This Topic

**Key Reason (story)**: 

- My friends invited me to go Hiking from Cusco to Machu Picchu, which is located in Peru. Gaining some prior knowledge before the trip.

- The country includes very different environments, such as the Amazon forest, the Andes, and coastal dry regions. Quite suitable for land cover change analysis.

## Research Question:


**"What were the major land cover changes in Peru from 2000 to 2020, and how did these land cover types change?"**


## Data:

- **Data source:** Copernicus Climate Data Store (CDS)  
- **Website:** https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/
- **Dataset:** ESA CCI / C3S annual land cover products (only focus on [lccs_class] layer)  
- **Study area:** Longitude: 82°W to 68°W Latitude: 19°S to 0°N (approximately)
- **Time span:** 2000–2020  
- **Spatial resolution:** 300 m 

## Method Overview {.smaller} 

1. **Prepare and reclassify annual land cover data**
   - Read annual files (2000–2020)
   - Extract the `lccs_class` layer
   - Crop and mask to Peru
   - Combine annual layers into `lc_stack`

2. **Build and visualize the land cover stack**
   - Create an animated overview of land cover change
   - To figure out major land cover change

3. **Apply category-specific trajectory analysis**
   - Create binary rasters for forest, cropland, and built-up
   - Using functions in `timeseriesTrajectories` to get plots
   - Analysis trajectories
  

## Step 1. Prepare and reclassify into new Land Cover Stack

```r
library(terra)
library(geodata)
library(here)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyterra)
library(sf)

# order and extract raw data
order_files <- c(
  sprintf("ESACCI-LC-L4-LCCS-Map-300m-P1Y-%s-v2.0.7cds.area-subset.0.-68.-19.-82.nc", 2000:2015),
  sprintf("C3S-LC-L4-LCCS-Map-300m-P1Y-%s-v2.1.1.area-subset.0.-68.-19.-82.nc", 2016:2020)
)
all_files <- here("extdata", order_files)
years <- 2000:2020

lc_reclassify <- function(lyr){
  # Define new classes
  cropland_vals <- c(10, 11, 12, 20, 30, 40)
  forest_vals <- c(50, 60, 61, 62, 70, 71, 72, 80, 81, 82, 90,160,170)
  builtup_vals <- c(190)
  water_vals <- c(210)
  ice_vals <- c(220)
  bare_vals <- c(200, 201, 202)
  grass_vals <- c(100, 110, 120, 121, 122, 130, 150, 151, 152, 153)
  wetland_vals <- c(180)

  # Reclassify
  new_lyr <- lyr
  values(new_lyr) <- 0
  new_lyr[lyr %in% cropland_vals] <- 1
  new_lyr[lyr %in% forest_vals] <- 2
  new_lyr[lyr %in% builtup_vals] <- 3
  new_lyr[lyr %in% water_vals] <- 4
  new_lyr[lyr %in% ice_vals] <- 5
  new_lyr[lyr %in% bare_vals] <- 6
  new_lyr[lyr %in% grass_vals] <- 7
  new_lyr[lyr %in% wetland_vals] <- 8

  return(new_lyr)
}

# Peru boundary
peru <- gadm(country = "PER", level = 0, path = tempdir())
con_layer <- rast(all_files[1])[["lccs_class"]]
peru <- project(peru, crs(con_layer))
peru_sf <- st_as_sf(peru)

# Build the annual land cover stack
all_layers <- lapply(1:length(all_files), function(i) {lc_rast <- rast(all_files[i])[["lccs_class"]]
  lc_reclass <- lc_reclassify(lc_rast)
  lc_peru <- mask(crop(lc_reclass, peru), peru)
  levels(lc_peru) <- data.frame(value = 1:8,
                                label = c("Cropland", "Forest", "Built-up", "Water","Snow/Ice",
                                          "Bare area", "Grassland", "Wetland"))
  names(lc_peru) <- paste0("year of", years[i])
  lc_peru
})
lc_stack <- rast(all_layers)

#Save the stack
writeRaster(
  lc_stack,
  here("processed_data", "lc_stack_2000_2020.tif"),
  overwrite = TRUE
)

```

## Step 2. Visualize Annual Land Cover Change

```r
library(terra)
library(geodata)
library(here)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyterra)
library(sf)
library(gganimate)

# Define a fixed color for each land cover category
fill_vals <- c(
  "Cropland" = "gold",
  "Forest" = "forestgreen",
  "Built-up" = "red",
  "Water" = "blue",
  "Snow/Ice" = "lightcyan",
  "Bare area" = "tan",
  "Grassland" = "yellowgreen",
  "Wetland" = "purple"
)

# Build the animated land cover map
anim <- ggplot() +

  # Draw the raster stack and assign category labels/colors
  geom_spatraster(
    data = lc_stack,
    aes(fill = factor(after_stat(value), levels = 1:8, labels = names(fill_vals)))
  ) +

  # Overlay the Peru boundary
  geom_sf(data = peru_sf, fill = NA, color = "black", linewidth = 0.2) +

  # Animate the layers over time
  transition_states(lyr, transition_length = 2, state_length = 1) +

  # Apply the manual color scale
  scale_fill_manual(values = fill_vals, na.translate = FALSE) +

  # Keep the spatial extent fixed
  coord_sf(expand = FALSE) +

  # Add the title and remove the legend title
  labs(title = "Peru Land Cover: {closest_state}", fill = NULL) +

  theme_bw()

# Export the animation as a GIF
animate(
  anim,
  nframes = 100,
  fps = 10,
  renderer = gifski_renderer(here("Project_Image", "peru_land_cover.gif"))
)
```

## Animated Overview of Land Cover Change 

<div style="text-align: center;">

![](../Project_Image/peru_land_cover.gif){width=60%}

</div>

<div class="small" style="text-align: center; margin-top: 0.3em;">

Annual land cover change in Peru from 2000 to 2020.

</div>


## Step 3. Prepare Inputs and do Trajectory Analysis

```r
library(timeseriesTrajectories)
library(ggplot2)
library(terra)
library(here)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyterra)
library(pdftools)

# Data preparation
lc_stack <- rast(here("processed_data", "lc_stack_2000_2020.tif"))
cropland_binary <- lc_stack == 1
cropland_area <- cropland_binary * cellSize(cropland_binary[[1]], unit = "km")
years <- 2000:2020
vert_units <- "Km2"
```

## Changes in Forest and Cropland Area Over Time {.analysis-slide}


<div class="small">

Plots were created with `plot_timeseries()` from the `timeseriesTrajectories` package.

</div>

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

### Cropland
![](../Project_Image/crop_plots/cropland_timeseries_area.png){width=88%}

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

### Forest
![](../Project_Image/forest_plots/forest_timeseries_change_area.png){width=88%}

:::
:::

- **Cropland** increased sharply around 2004 and then remained relatively stable, with a slight increase again in the late 2010s.
- **Forest** showed a decline around 2004, followed by a long period of relative stability and a modest recovery toward the end of the study period.

## Possible Explanation for Forest Loss Around 2003–2005

- New logging concessions may have contributed to the increase in forest disturbance around 2003–2005.
- Higher agricultural commodity prices may also have increased pressure on forest land.

<div class="small">
Source: Mongabay (2007), “Peru’s deforestation rate surged in 2005.”
</div>

## Change Trajectories: One-way Change {.traj-slide}

- **Gain without Alternation**  
  Appears and remains present.

- **Gain with Alternation**  
  Gains overall, with some back-and-forth change.

- **Loss without Alternation**  
  Disappears and does not return.

- **Loss with Alternation**  
  Loses overall, with some back-and-forth change.

### Examples
- `0 → 1 → 1` = Gain without Alternation
- `1 → 0 → 0` = Loss without Alternation
- `0 → 1 → 0 → 1` = Gain with Alternation (net change!!)
- `1 → 0 → 1 → 0` = Loss with Alternation (net change!!)


## Change Trajectories: Alternation {.traj-slide}



- **All Alternation Gain First**  

  The category first changes from absence to presence, then returns to the **original** state.

- **All Alternation Loss First**  

  The category first changes from presence to absence, then returns to the **original state**.

### Examples
- `0 → 1 → 0` = All Alternation Gain First (no net change!!)
- `1 → 0 → 1` = All Alternation Loss First (no net change!!)

## National-Scale Spatial Patterns {.traj-map-slide}

<div class="func-note">
Created with `plot_trajectory()` from the `timeseriesTrajectories` package
</div>

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

### Forest
![](../Project_Image/forest_plots/forest_traj_plot.png){width=92%}

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

### Cropland
![](../Project_Image/crop_plots/cropland_trajectory.png){width=92%}

:::
:::

- National maps show the **overall spatial structure** of change trajectories.
- **Forest** is dominated by stable presence in eastern Peru and stable absence in the west.
- **Cropland** is dominated by stable absence, with smaller and more fragmented areas of change.
- At the national scale, these maps show broad patterns.

## Zoomed-In Spatial Patterns of Change {.traj-map-slide}

<div class="func-note">
Zoomed-in trajectory maps created with `plot_trajectory()` from the `timeseriesTrajectories` package
</div>

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

### Forest
![](../Project_Image/forest_plots/forest_traj_plot_mask.png){width=96%}

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

### Cropland
![](../Project_Image/crop_plots/cropland_trajectory_mask.png){width=96%}

:::
:::

- The zoomed-in maps show that change is concentrated in **specific local areas**.
- **Forest** change appears mainly along forest edges and transition zones.
- **Cropland** change is more scattered and fragmented.
- Some forest-loss areas appear to align with cropland-gain areas, suggesting possible localized forest-to-cropland conversion.

## How to Read a Stacked Bar Trajectory Plot {.stackbar-slide}

<div class="func-note">
Created with `plot_stackbar()` from the `timeseriesTrajectories` package
</div>

<div style="text-align:center;">
![](../Project_Image/blank_stack_bar.png){width=45%}
</div>

- **Y-axis:** annual gross change, expressed as a percentage of the region
- **Vertical bar height:** the speed of change during each time interval
- **Horizontal bar width:** the duration of the time interval
- **Gross Gain / Gross Loss lines:** average change over the temporal extent

<div class="source-note">
Source: Bilintoh, Pontius Jr, and Zhang (2024), <em>Methods to compare sites concerning a category’s change during various time intervals</em>, explanation of Figure 4.
</div>

## Comparing Forest and Cropland Change Trajectories {.stackbar-compare-slide}

<div class="func-note">
Created with `plot_stackbar()` from the `timeseriesTrajectories` package
</div>

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

### Forest
![](../Project_Image/forest_plots/forest_stackbar_plot_1.png){width=97%}

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

### Cropland
![](../Project_Image/crop_plots/cropland_stackbar_plot_1.png){width=97%}

:::
:::

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

- **Forest:** strongest loss in **2000–2005** 
(**≈ -0.157**)
- **Forest:** strongest gain in **2015–2020** 
(**≈ +0.078**)
- **Forest gross change:** **Gain ≈ +0.035**, **Loss ≈ -0.047**

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

- **Cropland:** strongest gain in **2000–2005** (**≈ +1.299**)
- **Cropland:** strongest loss in **2000–2005** (**≈ -0.471**)
- **Cropland gross change:** **Gain ≈ +0.511**, **Loss ≈ -0.303**

:::
:::

- In both plots, **one-way gain/loss** is much larger than alternation.
- Forest and cropland show opposite overall trends: forest lost more than it gained, while cropland gained more than it lost, another evidence of localized forest-to-cropland conversion.
